school: The most celebrated judge (qadi) of early Islamic history; appointed judge of Kufa by Umar ibn al-Khattab and served for nearly 60 years through multiple caliphates; his legal acumen and witty judgments are the stuff of legend in Islamic juristic literature; model for all subsequent Islamic judges
parents: Zayd ibn Thabit / Jamila bint Sa'd ibn ar-Rabi'
spouses: Umama bint Jaliha b. 'Ubada b. 'Abdullah, Maymuna bint Hilal of B.Hilal

school: The greatest of the Tabieen (next generation) — never met the Prophet but loved him so deeply the Prophet said 'The fragrance of paradise comes from Yemen, from Uwais'; archetype of hidden sainthood in Sufism
quotes: I am amazed at the one who loves God yet disobeys Him — and the one who fears the Fire yet does not flee it.
The best of the servants of God are the hidden ones — those whose absence is not noticed and whose presence causes no pride.
Weep for yourself — for your time is passing and your deeds are few.

school: The primary student of Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud and effective head of the Kufan school of jurisprudence; teacher of Ibrahim al-Nakha'i who was Hammad's teacher who taught Abu Hanifa — making Alqama a crucial intellectual ancestor of the Hanafi school; known for strictness and precision in legal opinion
parents: 'Abdul 'Aziz bin Marwan / Umm Asim bint 'Asim bin 'Umar
children: 'Abdullah bin 'Umar bin 'Abdul 'Aziz , Zban bin 'Abdul 'Aziz bin Marwan , others

school: Nephew and student of Alqama ibn Qays; a major link in the Kufan hadith tradition from Ibn Mas'ud; also narrated directly from Aisha; known for fasting and worship; a critical ancestor in the chain leading to Abu Hanifa's school
quotes: I fasted until my face turned green and I prayed until my feet were swollen — and I do not regret a single moment.
Worship is not hardship when the heart is attached to God.

school: One of the most eminent Tabi'un scholars; became so close to Aisha that she treated him like a son; known for lengthy night prayers — he would prostrate so long his feet would swell; major transmitter of Kufan jurisprudence; his opinions on hadith methodology were extremely influential
quotes: It is sufficient knowledge for a man to fear God — and sufficient ignorance for a man to be impressed by his own knowledge.
The one who speaks much errs much — the one who errs much sins much.
Learn before you lead — for the leader who does not know leads others astray.

school: Freed slave of Umm al-Mu'minin Maymuna bint al-Harith; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina; known for his sound legal opinions and piety; famous for the story of refusing a woman's advances in the desert despite being alone with her — comparing himself to Yusuf

Son of Ali ibn Abi Talib by a wife named Khawla al-Hanafiyya; a respected scholar who refused to take sides in civil wars; some early Shia groups (the Kaysaniyya) believed he was the rightful imam; known for his legal opinions and avoidance of political strife; transmitted many hadiths

Hasan al-Basri (642–728) was the most influential scholar of the generation after the companions. Based in Basra, he was a towering figure in theology, ethics, and early Sufism. His circle produced the founders of both Mu’tazili rationalism and Sufi asceticism. Nearly every chain of Sufi transmission traces through him, making him the bridge between the companions and the later scholars.
parents: Abu al-Hasan / Khayra Umm al-Hasan al-'Abbassri
quotes: This world is three days: yesterday has gone with all that was in it; tomorrow you may never reach; but today is yours — so work in it.
Son of Adam, you will die alone, enter the grave alone, and be resurrected alone — so prepare yourself.
O son of Adam, you will die alone, enter the grave alone, be resurrected alone — so why do you live for others?
I have never regretted my silence — but I have often regretted my speech.
I have met people who feared their good deeds would be rejected more than others fear their sins.
This world has neither value nor weight with God — not even the weight of a mosquito's wing.
Son of Adam, you are a collection of days — each day that passes takes a part of you.

school: Considered the greatest of the Tabi'un generation; son-in-law of Abu Hurairah; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina whose opinions form the basis of Maliki law; famously imprisoned by the Umayyad governor for refusing to give an oath to their succession; his fatwas were unrivalled in authority
parents: al-Musayyib bin Hazn b. Abi Wahb / Umm Sa'id bint Sakhar bin Hakim
children: Muhammad bin Sa'id bin al-Musayyib , Sa'id, Ilyas, Umm 'Uthman, Umm 'Amr, Fakhta, Maryum
spouses: Umm Habib bint Abi Karim from Daus, Umm Walad
quotes: There is no goodness in a man who does not wish to increase in knowledge — and no goodness in a people who do not love those who teach them.
I have never missed the opening takbir of the dawn prayer in forty years.
There is no good in one who does not desire knowledge, and there is no good in knowledge that is not acted upon.
Whoever controls his tongue controls his honour.
The most truthful of people in their hope are the most frequent in their deeds.

school: The greatest exegete (mufassir) of the Tabi'un; read the entire Quran to Ibn Abbas thirty times, stopping at every verse to ask for its meaning — his tafsir represents the earliest authoritative interpretation; al-Shafi'i said: 'Whoever wants tafsir should go to Mujahid'; his exegesis is the primary source for all later commentators
quotes: I read the Quran to Ibn Abbas three times from beginning to end — stopping at every verse to ask what it meant.
Whoever is too proud to learn will remain in ignorance.

school: The first systematic historian of the Prophet's life; son of Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and nephew of Aisha; compiled the earliest organized collection of the Prophet's biography (sira) through chains from Aisha; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina; his letters to the Umayyad caliph form the earliest 'proto-history' of Islam
parents: 'Abdur Rahman ibn al-Harith / Fakhtah bint 'Unba bin Suhayl bin 'Amr
children: 'Abdullah bin Abi Bakr bin 'Abdur Rahman , 'Abdur Rahman bin Abi Bakr al-Mulaiki , 'Abdul Malik bin Abi Bakr bin 'AR , Hisham, Suhyal, al-Harith bin Abi Bakr , Maryam, Abu Salama, 'Umar bin Abi Bakr , Fatima
spouses: Sara bint Hisham Mukhzumi, Qurayba bint 'Abdullah Asadi, Ramisha bint Walid
quotes: Seek knowledge while you are young — for the tree is easier to shape while it is still a sapling.
Tell me your knowledge, and I will tell you mine — for two are wiser than one.

school: Freed slave of African origin who became the unrivaled religious authority of Mecca and the Hajj; the caliph Abdul Malik ibn Marwan sat at his feet for lessons; his student Ibn Jurayj became the first to systematically collect hadiths; the Prophet's tradition required: 'Take your religion from Ata'
quotes: A gathering for the remembrance of God is a gathering of forgiveness.
I met two hundred companions of the Prophet — and every one of them was an ocean of knowledge.

school: Freed slave and primary student of Ibn Abbas who dedicated his life to transmitting Ibn Abbas's vast knowledge of tafsir and hadith; his transmission of Ibn Abbas is essential to all Quranic exegesis; traveled extensively through the Islamic world to disseminate knowledge; some early scholars questioned his reliability but the majority accepted him
quotes: I learned from Ibn Abbas for fourteen years — and still felt I had only touched the surface.
The freed man who seeks knowledge is freer than the freeborn man who does not.
Every verse I interpreted, I interpreted only after I had learned its context.
I learned from Ibn Abbas for fourteen years — and still felt I had only touched the surface.
The freed man who seeks knowledge is freer than the freeborn man who does not.
Every verse I interpreted, I interpreted only after I had learned its context.

school: The foremost Islamic scholar of dream interpretation — his manual (Tafsir al-Ahlam) remains the most influential in the Islamic world; also a major hadith transmitter and jurist; freed slave of Anas ibn Malik; his piety was legendary — when he laughed by day he wept at night

books.title: Muntakhab al-Kalam fi Tafsir al-Ahlam (Dream Interpretation)
books.note: The most referenced Islamic work on dream interpretation

quotes: This knowledge is the religion — so be careful from whom you take your religion.
The dream of a believer is one of the forty-six parts of prophecy.
When you dream, look at the state your heart was in before you slept — for the dream follows the heart.
The one who guards his tongue guards himself from much evil.

school: The foremost jurist and ascetic of Yemen; student of 50 Companions including Ibn Abbas; so respected that the Umayyad caliph Hisham stood up for him — which he refused to acknowledge; known for his independence from rulers; master of the Yemeni legal school which influenced al-Shafi'i
quotes: I have accompanied the companions of the Prophet — and what I found most in them was silence.
The true jurist is the one who fears God — not the one who gives the most opinions.

school: Basran School — Direct disciple of Hasan al-Basri; first known non-Arab Sufi

famous: Qasim ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr
school: Grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq; raised by his grandmother Aisha after his father's death; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina whose opinions form the bedrock of Maliki jurisprudence; a key link in the chain of transmission from Aisha to Malik ibn Anas via Nafi'

famous: Salim ibn Abd Allah ibn Umar
school: Grandson of Umar ibn al-Khattab; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina; the primary transmitter of his father Ibn Umar's hadiths and practices; known for extreme piety and resemblance to his grandfather in character; his chain of transmission (Salim → Ibn Umar → Prophet) is among the most reliable in hadith

Son of Zayd ibn Thabit, the chief compiler of the Quran; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina; primary transmitter of his father's legal opinions and hadiths; his legal opinions are particularly valued in the areas of inheritance and marriage law which his father pioneered
parents: Zayd ibn Thabit / Jamila bint Sa'd ibn ar-Rabi'
children: Sulaiman bin Kharjh bin Zayd , 'Abdur Rahman bin Kharjh bin Zayd , Zayd, 'Umar, 'Abdullah bin Kharjh bin Zayd , Muhammad, Habiba, Hamida, Umm Yahya, Umm Suliaman
spouses: Umm 'Amr bint Hizm from B.Malik b. al-Najjar

famous: Ubayd Allah ibn Abd Allah ibn Utba
school: Grandnephew of Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud; one of the Seven Jurists of Medina; also a distinguished poet; teacher of scholars including al-Zuhri; known for combining legal rigor with literary gifts; one of the most authoritative links in the chain from Ibn Abbas

Freed slave who became the leading jurist of Syria; traveled widely seeking knowledge from hundreds of Companions and Tabi'un; the Syrian school of law derived from him; his independent legal opinions (ijtihad) were highly regarded; teacher of al-Awza'i who formalized the Syrian legal school

Freed slave and intimate student of Ibn Umar for 30 years; Malik ibn Anas used to say: 'If you want sound knowledge of Ibn Umar, go to Nafi''; the chain Malik → Nafi' → Ibn Umar → Prophet is called 'the golden chain' and is considered the most authentic chain of transmission in all of hadith scholarship
parents: Zayd ibn Thabit / Jamila bint Sa'd ibn ar-Rabi'
spouses: Bisama bint 'Ammarah b. Zayd from B.Malik b.al-Najjar

school: The most important figure in the formalization of hadith compilation; under Umayyad Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz he led the first state-sponsored hadith collection project; bridge between oral and written transmission; Malik ibn Anas and al-Bukhari both heavily relied on his chains; his chains are called 'golden' by hadith scholars
quotes: The transmission of knowledge is a duty — neglect it and the religion itself is neglected.
We did not wish to record knowledge in writing — but the princes compelled us, and so we wrote, for we feared it would be lost.
Knowledge does not give you all of itself at once — you must pursue it your whole life.
The Sunnah judges the opinion — the opinion does not judge the Sunnah.
Had it not been for the scholars, people would have been like animals.
Knowledge does not give you all of itself at once — you must pursue it your whole life.
The Sunnah judges the opinion — the opinion does not judge the Sunnah.
Had it not been for the scholars, people would have been like animals.

school: One of the most trusted hadith transmitters; refused to write hadiths down, relying entirely on memory; Ahmad ibn Hanbal praised him as trustworthy and reliable; particularly strong in hadiths from Anas ibn Malik and Abu Salama; his chains form a critical part of the six canonical hadith collections
parents: 'Abdur Rahman ibn al-Harith / Fakhtah bint 'Unba bin Suhayl bin 'Amr
children: 'Abdullah bin Ikramah bin 'Abdur Rahman , Muhammad bin 'Ikrama bin 'Abdur Rahman , 'Abduallah(Asghar), Harith, 'Uthman, Umm Sa'id
spouses: 'Atika bint 'Abdullah, Umm Salama bint 'Abdullah, Umm 'Abdur Rahman, Umm Walad

school: Born blind but became one of the greatest hadith scholars and Quran exegetes; never needed to write — he memorized everything he heard; one of the most trusted transmitters in Basra; Ahmad ibn Hanbal said: 'No one in Basra was more learned in hadith than Qatada'; key figure in both hadith and tafsir sciences
parents: 'Abdur Rahman ibn al-Harith / Fakhtah bint 'Unba bin Suhayl bin 'Amr
quotes: The Quran was revealed to be acted upon — make its commands your character.
There is no station higher than knowledge — and no station more dangerous.
The scholar who does not act on what he knows is a proof against himself.
The Quran was revealed to be acted upon — make its commands your character.
There is no station higher than knowledge — and no station more dangerous.
The scholar who does not act on what he knows is a proof against himself.

school: Medinian School — Teacher of both Imam Abu Hanifa (2 years) and Imam Malik; central link in all Sufi silsilas
quotes: Tell the truth even if it is against you.
He who has no intelligence has no manners; he who has no determination has no magnanimity; he who has no piety has no religion.
The generous person eats the food of others so they may eat his — the miser does not eat the food of others and they do not eat his.

school: Prophetic lineage — great-granddaughter of the Prophet; Imam al-Shafi'i was among her students; renowned for piety and scholarship

Persian mystic and Quran commentator (818–896) from Tustar in Khuzestan. His tafsir is one of the earliest surviving Sufi commentaries on the Quran. He developed the concept of the ‘light of Muhammad’ (nur Muhammadi) and the ‘covenant of the atoms’ — ideas that deeply influenced later Sufi theology. His students founded the Salimiyya school in Basra.
quotes: The world is ignorance and association with God is knowledge — the heart of the believer moves between these two.
Hold fast to three things: eating only what is lawful, fulfilling every obligation, and never abandoning the remembrance of God.

school: One of the foremost authorities among the Tābiʿīn and a towering pillar of Sunni hadith scholarship; Sufyan ibn ʿUyayna said he met 86 Tabi'in but none was like Ayyub. He studied under al-Hasan al-Basri, Nafī Mawlā Ibn ʿUmar, and Muhammad ibn Sirin, and was himself a teacher of Shuʿba ibn al-Ḥajjāj, Sufyan al-Thawri, and Ḥammād ibn Zayd. A resolute defender of Sunnah doctrine against early Muʿtazilite theology, he died in Basra during the great plague of 131 AH.

books.title: al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl fī Asmāʾ al-Rijāl
books.note: Definitive rijal reference with his teachers, students, and critical assessment

books.title: Ibn Ḥajar — Tahdhīb al-Tahdhīb
books.note: Key hadith-science reference for his classification and reliability

school: 8th-century jurist, ascetic, and Qurʾānic scholar from Basra; she memorised the entire Quran by age twelve and mastered all modes of recitation. Her brother Muhammad ibn Sirin — himself one of the greatest Tabi'un scholars — deferred to her on questions of Qurʾānic recitation. The judge Iyas ibn Muʿāwiya, asked to compare her to Hasan al-Basri and her own brother Muhammad, answered 'I do not prefer anyone over her.' Called a 'pioneer of female asceticism in Islam' by later scholars. Narrated 17 hadiths primarily from Anas ibn Malik and Umm Atiyya.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 7)
books.note: Biographical details on her piety, scholarship, and family connections

books.title: Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥusayn al-Sulamī — Dhikr al-Niswā al-Mutaʿabbidāt
books.note: Dedicated chapter on her as a pioneer of female Islamic asceticism

school: The foremost transmitter of Aisha's prophetic traditions; raised in Aisha's household after her father's death, she absorbed the complete corpus of Aisha's hadith knowledge. Al-Zuhrī called her 'an inexhaustible ocean' after his first visit; Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz ordered the official recording of her narrations alone. Her juridical rulings overrode male judges in Medina — as in the famous case of the Christian thief whose hand she prevented from being cut — accepted without challenge by the sitting judge. Imam Sufyan ibn ʿUyayna ranked her alongside al-Qasim ibn Muhammad and Urwah ibn al-Zubayr as the three most authoritative transmitters of Aisha's hadiths.

books.title: Imam Mālik — al-Muwaṭṭaʾ
books.note: Contains the famous khulʿ case and other key rulings traced through Amrah

books.title: Ibn Ḥajar — Tahdhīb al-Tahdhīb
books.note: Full rijal assessment confirming her authority as a hadith transmitter

school: Daughter of Talha ibn Ubaydillah — one of the Ten Companions Promised Paradise — and niece of Aisha bint Abi Bakr through her mother Umm Kulthum bint Abi Bakr. She grew up in close contact with her famous aunt and became a significant transmitter of Aisha's hadith corpus. Her name appears as a key node in the isnad network linking the household of the first Caliph Abu Bakr to the second and third hadith generations. She is cited as a narrator by major successors including Muawiya ibn Ishaq and Ibn Abi Najih.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)
books.note: Biographical entry on the daughters of the companions

books.title: al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl fī Asmāʾ al-Rijāl
books.note: Rijal reference confirming her as a reliable female narrator

books.title: Ibn Ḥajar — Tahdhīb al-Tahdhīb
books.note: Assessment of her place in the hadith transmission chain

school: Son of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf — one of the Ten Companions Promised Paradise — and a leading Medinan jurist counted among the Seven Jurists of Medina (al-Fuqahāʾ al-Sabʿa) whose legal opinions formed the foundation of Medinan and Maliki jurisprudence. He transmitted extensively from Aisha, Umm Salamah, Abu Hurayrah, and other companions, and was himself a teacher of Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri, Yahya ibn Said al-Ansari, and Muhammad ibn Ibrahim al-Taymi.

books.title: al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl fī Asmāʾ al-Rijāl
books.note: Comprehensive rijal entry listing his teachers, students, and reliability

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 5)
books.note: Primary biographical source for the second generation Medinan scholars

school: Granddaughter of Asma bint Abi Bakr and great-niece of Aisha bint Abi Bakr; married to the prominent hadith scholar Hisham ibn Urwah ibn al-Zubayr (her cousin). She transmitted traditions from her grandmother Asma bint Abi Bakr, forming a key isnad link connecting the household of Abu Bakr to the third hadith generation through the prominent Zubayr family of Medina. Her narrations appear in the major hadith collections transmitted by her husband Hisham.

books.title: al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl fī Asmāʾ al-Rijāl
books.note: Rijal entry identifying her place in the isnad chains through Hisham ibn Urwah

books.title: Ibn Ḥajar — Tahdhīb al-Tahdhīb
books.note: Scholarly classification and assessment of her narrations

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)
books.note: Biographical notes on the women of the Zubayr family lineage

school: Wife of the famous companion Abdullah ibn Umar ibn al-Khattab; through her marriage she was positioned at the nexus of hadith transmission connecting the household of Umar ibn al-Khattab to the second generation. She transmitted from Abdullah ibn Umar, Aisha bint Abi Bakr, and Hafsa bint Umar (her sister-in-law), and her narrations appear in the major collections. Her family's lineage from the Thaqif tribe and her husband's eminent status as one of the most prolific hadith narrators gave her traditions particular weight.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)
books.note: Biographical details on the wives and daughters in the Umar ibn al-Khattab household chain

books.title: Ibn ʿAbd al-Barr — al-Istīʿāb fī Maʿrifat al-Aṣḥāb
books.note: Reference entry confirming her marriage to Abdullah ibn Umar and her narrations

books.title: al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl fī Asmāʾ al-Rijāl
books.note: Rijal assessment of her reliability as a hadith transmitter

school: Son of Husayn ibn Ali and grandson of Ali ibn Abi Talib — the sole adult male survivor of the massacre of Karbala (680 CE / 61 AH). He was gravely ill during the battle and could not fight; Zaynab bint Ali, his aunt, is credited with shielding him from execution by the forces of Yazid. He was taken as a prisoner to Kufa and then to Damascus, where his composed dignity and the powerful speech of Zaynab turned the tide of public opinion against Yazid. After release he returned to Madinah where he lived the rest of his life in extraordinary piety, scholarship, and ascetic devotion. He authored al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya — a collection of 54 supplicatory prayers (du'as) that is considered one of the greatest works of Islamic devotional literature, called 'the Psalms of the House of Muhammad.' His prayers cover every dimension of human spiritual experience — gratitude, repentance, morning and evening supplications, prayers for parents and children — and are recited by millions today across sectarian lines. He spent entire nights in prayer and was seen to weep constantly; when asked why he wept so much he said: 'Jacob wept for one lost son until he lost his sight — I have lost eighteen of my family in a single day.' He transmitted extensive hadith and his students include al-Zuhri and other major Tabi'un scholars. He is the crucial link between Husayn ibn Ali and the continuing Imam tradition, and between the tragedy of Karbala and the living spiritual chain.
quotes: The rights of God upon you are that you worship Him and associate nothing with Him.
Be a son of the hereafter and not a son of this world — for the son returns to his father.
I wonder at the one who is proud — he was a drop of fluid yesterday and will be a corpse tomorrow.

school: Son of Ali ibn al-Husayn (Zayn al-Abidin) and the teacher of Jafar al-Sadiq — the critical middle link in the chain from Husayn ibn Ali (in this database) to Jafar al-Sadiq (in this database). His title al-Baqir means 'the one who splits open knowledge' — referring to his ability to unlock the inner meanings of the Quran and prophetic tradition. He was present as a child at Karbala (680 CE) and witnessed the massacre. He spent his life in Madinah as the foremost religious authority of the Alid family, teaching an extraordinarily wide circle of students from all schools. His students include Jabir ibn Hayyan (the founder of chemistry who transmitted scientific knowledge from him), major jurists of the Sunni schools, and hundreds of hadith transmitters. He systematised Quranic exegesis (tafsir) and jurisprudence in ways that influenced all subsequent Islamic scholarship — Imam Abu Hanifa reportedly said: 'I have not seen anyone more learned in jurisprudence than Muhammad al-Baqir.' He composed no single famous text but his teachings were recorded by hundreds of students and form the foundation of Jafari jurisprudence. The Jabir ibn Hayyan connection makes him a node between Islamic spiritual and scientific knowledge — his teachings were transmitted into the early development of Islamic chemistry, alchemy, and natural philosophy.
quotes: Our follower is not one who merely speaks our words — he is one who acts on them.
Knowledge is the companion of the believer — patience is his minister — forbearance is his father.
Whoever his tongue does not guard, his religion is not safe.
